title: Batching

content.question: What are you doing scattered that you could batch once a week?
sop: List everything you did yesterday and tag each task by type (creative, admin, communication, etc.).
Count how many times you switched task type, that's your Switching count (see calculation, page 4)
Group similar tasks from the coming week into one or two dedicated blocks.
Protect each block from interruption, no email, no messages, no other task types.
Track Output completed per block to confirm batching is actually working (see calculation, page 4)
questions: What are you doing scattered that you could batch once a week?
How many times today did you switch between fundamentally different types of task?
What would change if you only checked messages twice a day instead of continuously?
content.equation_example: Output equals 12 (tasks completed), Switching equals 4 (number of times you changed task type). Efficiency equals 3 outputs per switch. Higher means you're completing more before context-switching drains you. Lower means you're bleeding time re-orienting between tasks instead of doing them.
content.closing_statement: Every switch costs more than the task itself.

caption:
Principle Nº 138: Batching

Switching tasks kills efficiency.

Ask yourself: What are you doing scattered that you could batch once a week?
